title: AQ Podcast | A New Criminal Landscape in Latin America
gist: An Americas Quarterly podcast episode description frames 2026 as a security-defined moment in Latin American politics — hardline crackdown wins in Colombia and Ecuador and the Trump administration's new 'Shield of the Americas' coalition — previewing a discussion with InSight Crime's Jeremy McDermott on whether militarized enforcement is actually working as organized crime keeps diversifying into new markets.

insights.claim: Security has become the defining issue in Latin American politics, evidenced by two 2026 presidential wins built on hardline platforms: Abelardo de la Espriella in Colombia, who campaigned on scrapping Petro's 'total peace' plan, and Daniel Noboa's re-election in Ecuador on the back of a military crackdown against criminal gangs.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: Confirms a region-wide electoral realignment toward militarized security policy — a leading indicator for policy continuity and security-sector risk across Latin America.
insights.locator: Security has become the defining issue

insights.claim: The Trump administration has launched the 'Shield of the Americas,' a coalition of like-minded governments committed to a military-led approach to fighting organized crime in Latin America.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: A concrete new U.S. security initiative to track for defense-cooperation and regional security-spending exposure.
insights.locator: in Washington, the Trump administration launched

insights.claim: Despite the region's militarized crackdown, criminal groups continue reorganizing and diversifying into new markets, from the United States and Europe to an increasingly lucrative market in Asia and within Latin America itself.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: Suggests militarized enforcement may be displacing rather than eliminating criminal revenue streams, implying persistent security risk despite crackdown headlines.
insights.quote: reorganizing, diversifying, and finding new markets to tap into
insights.locator: Meanwhile, criminal groups are doing what they always do

insights.claim: The episode frames two central open questions for the region's security outlook: how well the militarized approach against organized crime is actually working, and what to expect from the new generation of right-wing security-focused leaders.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: Sets the analytical lens — efficacy of militarization — that should be applied when evaluating claimed security gains under Noboa, De La Espriella, and similar leaders.
insights.quote: How well is the militarized approach working?
